1: ûḇišnêm ʿāśār ḥōḏeš hûʾ-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār bišlōšāh ʿāśār yôm bô — 'And in the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar, on the thirteenth day of it.' The date formula opens the battle narrative with precision: the day Haman chose by lot in 3:7 (from the first month of the year through all twelve months, the lot fell on Adar 13) is now the day of the Jews' counter-defense. ʾăšer higgîaʿ diḇar-hammeleḵ wədāṯô laʿăśôṯ bô — 'when the word of the king and his edict were about to be executed.' Both decrees are operative simultaneously on this day: the original decree (destruction of Jews) and the counter-decree (authorization of self-defense). bəyôm ʾăšer śibərû ʾôyəḇê hayyəhûḏîm lišlôṭ bāhem wənāhăp̄ôḵ hûʾ ʾăšer yišlְṭû hayyəhûḏîm hēmmāh bəśōnəʾêhem — 'on the day when the enemies of the Jews hoped to overpower them, but it was reversed (nāhăp̄ôḵ): the Jews themselves overpowered those who hated them.' Hāp̄ak (H2015 = to turn over, reverse, overturn) — the verb of reversal used of the Sodom overthrow (Gen 19:25), the sea-crossing (Exod 14:27), and divine repentance. The entire narrative program of Esther is compressed into this one verb.
2: niqqahălû hayyəhûḏîm bəʿārêhem bəḵol-məḏînôṯ hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš lišlōaḥ yāḏ bimbaqqəšê rāʿāṯām — 'The Jews assembled in their cities throughout all the provinces of King Ahasuerus to lay hands on those who sought their harm.' Lišlōaḥ yāḏ (to send/lay the hand, i.e., to attack) — the same idiom used of Haman's intended violence against the Jews. The Jews' legal authorization mirrors Haman's action precisely: they do to their attackers what Haman's decree authorized to be done to them. wəʾîš lōʾ-ʿāmaḏ lip̄nêhem kî-nāpal paḥdām ʿal-kol-hāʿammîm — 'and no one could withstand them, for dread of them had fallen on all the peoples.' Nāpal paḥad (fear fell) — the same formula as 8:17 (fear of the Jews fell upon the peoples) and echoes the Rahab passage (Josh 2:9 — the same formula used of Israel's arrival in Canaan).
3: wəḵol-śārê hammĕḏînôṯ wəhāʾăḥašdarpənîm wəhappaḥôṯ wəʿōśê hamməlāʾḵāh ʾăšer lammeleḵ mənaśśəʾîm ʾeṯ-hayyəhûḏîm — 'All the officials of the provinces, the satraps, the governors, and those doing the king's business supported the Jews.' Nāśāʾ (H5375 = to lift, support, favor) — the provincial administration's active support is not merely passive tolerance but active facilitation. kî-nāpal paḥaḏ Mordoḵay ʿălêhem — 'for dread of Mordecai had fallen on them.' The fear-formula is now personalized to Mordecai: the political actor whose elevation and counter-decree created the new situation.
4: kî-gāḏôl Mordoḵay bêṯ hammeleḵ wəšimʿô hôlēḵ bəḵol-hammĕḏînôṯ kî hāʾîš Mordoḵay hôlēḵ wəgāḏôl — 'for Mordecai was great in the king's house, and his fame spread throughout all the provinces, for the man Mordecai was growing greater and greater.' The doubled greatness statement — gāḏôl in both clauses, with hôlēḵ wəgāḏôl (going/becoming ever greater, a Hebrew idiom for continuous progression) — signals that Mordecai's position has reached empire-wide recognition. The phrase hôlēḵ wəgāḏôl uses the participle of motion (hālaḵ, to walk) with gāḏôl as a complementary predicate — a standard Hebrew idiom for continuous increase (cf. Gen 26:13; 2 Sam 3:1).
5: wayyakkû hayyəhûḏîm bəḵol-ʾôyəḇêhem makkat-ḥereḇ wəhereḡ wəʾăḇāḏan wayyaʿăśû bəśōnəʾêhem kirṣônām — 'The Jews struck all their enemies with the stroke of the sword, killing and destroying, and they did to those who hated them as they pleased.' Makkat-ḥereḇ (H4347 = blow of the sword) — the military engagement idiom. Wəhereḡ wəʾăḇāḏan (killing and destroying) — two of the three terms from the original destruction decree (3:13), now performed by the Jews against their attackers. Kirṣônām (H7522 = according to their will/pleasure) — the Jews act with full authorization and capacity.
6: ûḇəšûšan habbîrāh hārgû hayyəhûḏîm wəʾabbēḏ ḥămiššah mēʾôṯ ʾîš — 'In Susa the citadel the Jews killed and destroyed 500 men.' The specific number in the capital — 500 — is followed by the list of Haman's ten sons (vv7-9), making the capital's battle focused on the Agagite family that originated the threat. The killing in the capital is the resolution of the central conflict; the deaths in the provinces (vv12-15) are the extension.
7: wəʾēṯ Paršandāṯāʾ wəʾēṯ Dalp̄ôn wəʾēṯ ʾAspāṯāʾ — 'and Parshandatha and Dalphon and Aspatha.' The list of Haman's ten sons begins here and continues through v9. All ten names are Persian or non-Hebrew in origin — most scholars identify them as Persian or possibly Elamite names. The enumeration of ten sons in three verses (7-9) creates a litany of the destroyed Agagite line; the Amalekite enemy, whose extermination Saul failed to complete (1 Sam 15), is now fully eliminated.
8: wəʾēṯ Pôraṯāʾ wəʾēṯ ʾAḏalyāʾ wəʾēṯ ʾArîḏāṯāʾ — 'and Poratha and Adalia and Aridatha.' The continuation of the son-list. ʾAḏalyāʾ has been proposed to contain a theophoric element (-yah), though this is debated. The names' foreignness marks the sons as unambiguously Persian-Agagite, not Jewish.
9: wəʾēṯ Parmašṯāʾ wəʾēṯ ʾArîsay wəʾēṯ ʾArîḏay wəʾēṯ Wayzāṯāʾ — 'and Parmashta and Arisai and Aridai and Vaizatha.' The final four sons complete the ten. The talmudic tradition (Megillah 16b) notes that the scribes wrote all ten names in a single column (rather than across a line), and that the three letters vav (ו) in three names should be written large — encoding a visual memory-device for the tradition of the ten hanged sons. The ten sons are killed but not plundered (v10), echoing Saul's failure: here Israel does what Saul did not.
10: ʿăśeṯ bənê-Hāmān ben-Hammedāṯāʾ ṣōrēr hayyəhûḏîm hāragû ûḇabizzāh lōʾ šālāḥû ʾeṯ-yāḏām — 'the ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews, they killed, but on the plunder they did not lay their hands.' The refusal of plunder is the detail that most clearly echoes the Amalekite war of 1 Sam 15 — where Saul and the people kept the plunder against YHWH's command (ḥērem), leading to Saul's rejection. Here the Jews voluntarily refuse the plunder that the counter-decree authorized (8:11), demonstrating that their purpose is not enrichment but survival. The contrast is a deliberate narrative correction of Saul's failure.
11: bayyôm hahûʾ bāʾ mispar hahaRûgîm bəšûšan habbîrāh lip̄nê hammeleḵ — 'On that day the number of those killed in Susa the citadel was brought before the king.' The formal report to the king maintains the narrative's bureaucratic framework — even the battle results are processed through Persian administrative procedure. wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ ləʾEsṯēr hammalkāh — 'And the king said to Queen Esther.' The king's immediate response is to inform Esther, centering her in the narrative even after the action has shifted to the military sphere.
12: bəšûšan habbîrāh hārgû hayyəhûḏîm wəʾabbēḏ ḥămiššāh mēʾôṯ ʾîš wəʾēṯ ʿăśeṯ bənê-Hāmān bišʾār məḏînôṯ hammeleḵ māh ʿāśû — 'In Susa the citadel the Jews have killed 500 men and the ten sons of Haman; in the remaining provinces of the king what have they done?' The king's rhetorical question acknowledges that the full scope of the battle is beyond even his immediate knowledge — the empire is too large for same-day reporting. ûmah-šəʾēlāṯēḵ wəyinnāṭen lāḵ ûmāh-baqqāšāṯēḵ ʿôḏ wəṯēʿāś — 'What further is your request and it shall be granted, and what more is your petition and it shall be done.' The king's unlimited offer (the royal formula) is repeated — but now Esther has already achieved the primary goal; her second request is for an additional day.
13: watōʾmer ʾEsṯēr ʾim-ʿal-hammeleḵ ṭôḇ yinnāṯēn gam-māḥār layyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bəšûšān laʿăśôṯ kədāṯ hayyôm — 'Esther said: If it pleases the king, let it be granted to the Jews who are in Susa to do tomorrow also according to the edict of today.' The additional day request is for Susa specifically — the capital, where the political opponents (Haman's allies? Agagite sympathizers?) are concentrated. wəʾēṯ ʿăśeṯ bənê-Hāmān yitlû ʿal-hāʿēṣ — 'and let the ten sons of Haman be hanged on the gallows.' Though already killed (vv7-9), the public hanging of their bodies is requested as deterrent display — ANE practice of exhibiting slain enemies publicly (cf. Josh 10:26).
14: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ lhēʿāśôṯ kēn — 'And the king commanded that it be done.' The bare command, without elaboration, signals the king's continued compliance. wattinnāṭēn dāṯ bəšûšān wayyillu ʾēṯ ʿăśeṯ bənê-Hāmān — 'an edict was given in Susa, and they hanged the ten sons of Haman.' The passive wattinnāṭēn (it was given) sustains the book's indirect-agency style.
15: wayyiqqāhălû hayyəhûḏîm ʾăšer-bəšûšān gam bəyôm ʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār lə-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār — 'The Jews who were in Susa assembled also on the fourteenth day of Adar.' The second day's battle in Susa extends the fighting one day beyond the rest of the empire. wayyahargû bəšûšān šlōšet mēʾôṯ ʾîš ûḇabizzāh lōʾ šālăḥû ʾeṯ-yāḏām — 'They killed 300 men in Susa, but they did not lay their hands on the plunder.' The refusal of plunder is repeated — the second statement in the chapter (cf. v10) — emphasizing that the no-plunder policy is consistent and intentional, not circumstantial.
16: ûšəʾār hayyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bimdinôṯ hammeleḵ niqqahălû wəʿāmōḏ ʿal-napšām — 'The rest of the Jews who were in the king's provinces assembled and stood for their lives.' Wəʿāmōḏ ʿal-napšām (to stand for their lives) — the phrase from the counter-decree (8:11) is fulfilled: the Jews did precisely what they were authorized to do. wənôaḥ mēʾôyəḇêhem wəhārgû bəśōnəʾêhem ḥămiššāh wəšibʿîm ʾelep̄ ûḇabizzāh lōʾ šālăḥû ʾeṯ-yāḏām — 'and had rest from their enemies and killed 75,000 of those who hated them, but they did not lay their hands on the plunder.' Nôaḥ mēʾôyəḇêhem (rest from enemies, H5117 + H341) — the Deuteronomic rest formula (Deut 12:10; 25:19; Josh 21:44; 23:1) that signaled the completion of the conquest and the fulfillment of the land promise. The use of this formula here is theologically laden: the Diaspora Jews experience a form of the Deuteronomic 'rest' — relief from the threat of enemies — even outside the land.
17: bəyôm šəlōšāh ʿāśār lə-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār ûbəʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār bô — 'On the thirteenth day of the month of Adar and on the fourteenth day of it.' The two-day battle sequence (13th in the provinces; 13th–14th in Susa) produces the calendrical difference that will explain why different communities celebrate Purim on different dates (v18-19). wənôaḥ bəʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār bô wəʿāśôh ʾōṯô yôm mišṭeh wəśimḥāh — 'They rested on the fourteenth day and made it a day of feasting and rejoicing.' The rest-from-enemies followed by a feast-day maps onto the liturgical pattern: the Sabbath rests from labor and observes joy; here the Jews rest from battle and observe joy.
18: wəhayyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bəšûšān niqqahălû bišlōšāh ʿāśār bô ûbəʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār bô wənôaḥ bəḥămiššāh ʿāśār bô wəʿāśôh ʾōṯô yôm mišṭeh wəśimḥāh — 'The Jews who were in Susa assembled on the thirteenth and fourteenth, and rested on the fifteenth; they made it a day of feasting and rejoicing.' The Susa Jews fought on both the 13th and 14th (the extra day Esther requested), rested on the 15th, and celebrate on the 15th. Hence two Purim dates: 14 Adar for the provincial cities (the 'open cities' or ʿārê happərāzôṯ, unwalled), 15 Adar for the walled cities (Susa and subsequently walled cities in Israel).
19: ʿal-kēn hayyəhûḏîm happərāzîm hayyōšəḇîm bəʿārê happərāzôṯ ʿōśîm ʾēṯ yôm ʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār lə-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār śimḥāh ûmišṭeh wəyôm ṭôḇ ûmišlôaḥ mānôṯ ʾîš lərēʿēhû — 'Therefore the rural Jews who live in the open villages keep the fourteenth day of the month of Adar as a day of gladness and feasting and holiday and for sending gifts of food to one another.' The term happərāzîm (H6521 = those who live in unwalled villages, open settlements) distinguishes the community type: no walls = 14 Adar; walled city = 15 Adar (v18). Mišlôaḥ mānôṯ (H4916 + H4490 = sending of portions/gifts of food) — the practice of sending portions to neighbors becomes a defining Purim practice. This reciprocal food-sharing is the social enactment of the community solidarity that made survival possible.
20: wayyiḵtōḇ Mordoḵay ʾeṯ-hadəḇārîm hāʾēlleh wayyišlaḥ səp̄ārîm ʾel-kol-hayyəhûḏîm ʾăšer bəḵol-məḏînôṯ hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš haqərôḇîm wəhārəḥôqîm — 'Mordecai recorded these things and sent letters to all the Jews in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus, both near and far.' Wayyiḵtōḇ Mordoḵay ʾeṯ-hadəḇārîm hāʾēlleh — 'Mordecai wrote these words' — a possible self-referential notice that Mordecai is the author/source of at least part of the Esther narrative. Haqərôḇîm wəhārəḥôqîm (the near and the far) is a merism covering all Jews in the Diaspora.
21: ləqayyēm ʿălêhem lihyôṯ ʿōśîm ʾēṯ yôm ʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār lə-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār wəʾēṯ yôm-ḥămiššāh ʿāśār bô bəḵol-šānāh wəšānāh — 'to obligate them to observe annually the fourteenth and fifteenth days of Adar.' Ləqayyēm (H6965, piel = to establish, confirm, ratify) — Mordecai's letters do not merely describe a past celebration but institute a perpetual obligation. Bəḵol-šānāh wəšānāh (in every year and year) — the distributive repetition emphasizes annual, unbroken observance across all future generations.
22: kayyāmîm ʾăšer-nāḥû ḇāhem hayyəhûḏîm mēʾôyəḇêhem wəhaḥōḏeš ʾăšer nehpak lāhem miYyāgôn ləśimḥāh ûmēʾēḇel ləyôm ṭôḇ — 'as the days on which the Jews had rest from their enemies, and the month that had been turned for them from grief to gladness and from mourning into a holiday.' The theological summary of Purim: the month of Adar was the month Haman chose for destruction — it has been nehpak (H2015, niphal = turned over, reversed) from sorrow to joy. MiYyāgôn ləśimḥāh ûmēʾēḇel ləyôm ṭôḇ (from grief to gladness, from mourning to holiday) — paired antitheses that define the feast as the annual commemoration of reversal. laʿăśôṯ ʾôṯām yəmê mišṭeh wəśimḥāh ûmišlôaḥ mānôṯ ʾîš lərēʿēhû ûmattānôṯ lāʾeḇyônîm — 'to observe them as days of feasting and gladness, sending gifts of food to one another and gifts to the poor.' The three Purim practices: feasting, sending portions to neighbors, giving gifts to the poor (ʾeḇyônîm, H34).
23: wəqibbēl hayyəhûḏîm ʾēṯ ʾăšer-hēḥēllû laʿăśôṯ wəʾēṯ ʾăšer-kāṯaḇ Mordoḵay ʾălêhem — 'So the Jews undertook to do as they had started and as Mordecai had written to them.' Qibbēl (H6901, piel = to receive, accept, undertake) — the community's voluntary acceptance of the feast obligation. The combination of spontaneous celebration (what they 'had started' doing) and Mordecai's written institutionalization creates the feast's dual foundation: organic commemoration and official legislation.
24: kî Hāmān ben-Hammedāṯāʾ hāʾăgāgî ṣōrēr kol-hayyəhûḏîm ḥāšaḇ ʿal-hayyəhûḏîm ləʾabbəḏām wəhippîl pûr hûʾ-haggôrāl ləhumām ûləʾabbəḏām — 'for Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, the enemy of all the Jews, had planned against the Jews to destroy them and had cast Pur — that is, the lot — to crush and destroy them.' The narrative summary of chapters 3-7 is condensed into one sentence for the feast's etiological explanation. The parenthetical gloss hûʾ-haggôrāl (that is, the lot) explains the Akkadian loanword pûr (H6332) — a word that non-Hebrew speaking diaspora Jews might not recognize — and by naming the lot, the feast name Purim is explained: the feast commemorates the overthrow of the lot that Haman cast against the Jews.
25: ûḇəḇōʾāh lip̄nê hammeleḵ ʾāmar ʿim-hassēper yāšûḇ maḥăšaḇtô hārāʿāh ʾăšer ḥāšaḇ ʿal-hayyəhûḏîm ʿal-rōʾšô — 'but when Esther came before the king, he commanded in writing that his wicked plan which he devised against the Jews should return upon his own head.' The reversal is stated in the clearest theological form: Haman's plan returned (yāšûḇ, H7725 = to return) upon his own head. The structure is the lex talionis in narrative form: what was plotted against others fell upon the plotter. wəṯālû ʾōṯô wəʾeṯ-bānāyw ʿal-hāʿēṣ — 'and that they should hang him and his sons on the gallows.' The hanging of Haman and his sons is the final execution of the reversal.
26: ʿal-kēn qārəʾû layyāmîm hāʾēlleh Pûrîm ʿal-šēm happûr — 'Therefore these days were called Purim, from the word Pur.' The feast's name is explicitly derived from the Akkadian loanword: pûr (lot) → pûrîm (the plural). The feast is named after the instrument of intended destruction — a way of commemorating the instrument while celebrating its failure. This is the book's only explicit etymology. ʿal-kēn ʿal-kol-diḇrê haʾiggereṯ hazzōʾṯ — 'according to all the words of this letter.' The reference to haʾiggereṯ hazzōʾṯ (this letter/document) is a self-referential textual marker, indicating that the Esther scroll itself has this letter-form function.
27: qiymû wəqibbēl hayyəhûḏîm ʿălêhem wəʿal-zaraʿām wəʿal kol-hannilvîm ʿălêhem — 'The Jews established and accepted for themselves and their descendants and all who joined them.' The tripartite community who undertakes the feast obligation: the Jews themselves, their descendants (zaraʿam), and the proselytes who 'joined themselves' to Israel (hannilvîm, H3867, niphal = those who have attached themselves, a technical term for proselytes or those who identify with Israel, cf. Isa 14:1; 56:3,6). The feast is open to those who join Israel by choice, not only those born into it. wəlōʾ yaʿăḇōr lihyôṯ ʿōśîm ʾēṯ šənê hayyāmîm hāʾēlleh ḵikəṯāḇām wəḵizzəmannām bəḵol-šānāh wəšānāh — 'that without fail they would observe these two days according to their writing and their time every year.' The double negation lōʾ yaʿăḇōr (will not pass over/transgress) parallels the language of unbreakable covenantal obligation.
28: wəhayyāmîm hāʾēlleh nizkārîm wənaʿăśîm bəḵol-dôr wāḏôr bəḵol-mišpāḥāh ûmišpāḥāh bəḵol-məḏînāh ûməḏînāh ûbəḵol-ʿîr wāʿîr — 'These days should be remembered and kept throughout every generation, every clan, every province, and every city.' The four-level distribution — generation, family, province, city — exhausts all categories of human community: temporal (generations), familial (clans), political (provinces), urban (cities). Nizkārîm wənaʿăśîm (remembered and observed) — the two pillars of perpetual feast observance: internal memorial and external practice. wîmê hapPûrîm hāʾēlleh lōʾ yaʿăḇərû mittôḵ hayyəhûḏîm wəziḵrām lōʾ-yāsûp̄ mizzarʿām — 'these Purim days will never cease among the Jews, nor will the remembrance of them perish from their descendants.' The double assurance of perpetuity — lōʾ yaʿăḇərû (will not pass away) and lōʾ-yāsûp̄ (will not end) — grounds the feast in the permanence of the events that prompted it.
29: wattiktoḇ ʾEsṯēr hammalkāh baṯ-ʾAḇîḥayil wəMordoḵay hayyəhûḏî ʾeṯ-kol-tōqep̄ ləqayyēm ʾēṯ ʾiggereṯ hapPûrîm haззōʾṯ haššēnîṯ — 'Queen Esther daughter of Abihail and Mordecai the Jew wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter of Purim.' The joint authorship of the second Purim letter — Esther named first, then Mordecai — is significant: Esther acts not merely as Mordecai's instrument but as an authority in her own right. Baṯ-ʾAḇîḥayil (daughter of Abihail) — the only place in the book where Esther's father's name is given (cf. 2:15), marking this as a formal legal document that requires full identification. Tōqep̄ (H8633 = authority, power, validity) — the legal term for the binding force of a written document.
30: wayyišlaḥ səp̄ārîm ʾel-kol-hayyəhûḏîm ʾel šeḇaʿ wəʿeśrîm ûmēʾāh məḏînāh malkûṯ ʾAḥašwērôš diḇrê šālôm weʾěmeṯ — 'He sent letters to all the Jews in the 127 provinces of the kingdom of Ahasuerus with words of peace and truth.' Diḇrê šālôm weʾěmeṯ (words of peace and truth/faithfulness, H7965 + H571) — the paired attributes of the Mosaic covenant relationship (Zech 8:19) and the divine character (Exod 34:6). The phrase frames the Purim decree as aligned with covenant values: the feast celebrates not merely political survival but the continuation of the covenant community.
31: ləqayyēm ʾēṯ-yəmê hapPûrîm hāʾēlleh biizmənêhem kaʾăšer qiyyam ʿălêhem Mordoḵay hayyəhûḏî wəʾEsṯēr hammalkāh — 'to confirm these Purim days at their proper times, as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther established them.' The confirmation formula repeats qiyyam (H6965, piel = to establish, ratify, confirm) — used three times in the Purim-establishment passage (vv27,28,31) — creating a triple ratification. wəḵaʾăšer qiyyamû ʿal-nap̄šām wəʿal-zarʿām diḇrê haṣṣômôṯ wəzaʿăqāṯām — 'and as they had established for themselves and their descendants the matters of the fastings and their lamenting.' The reference to fasting (ṣômôṯ, H6685) and crying out (zaʿăqāṯām, H2201) points back to the communal mourning of 4:3 — the fast Esther called in 4:16. The Purim feast is the inverse of the fast: the community that fasted and mourned now feasts and rejoices.
32: ûmaʾămar ʾEsṯēr qiyyam diḇrê hapPûrîm hāʾēlleh wəniḵtaḇ bəsēfer — 'The command of Esther established these Purim practices, and it was recorded in the book.' Maʾămar ʾEsṯēr (Esther's command/word, H3982) — the book closes the feast-legislation with Esther's authority as its ultimate human basis. Wəniḵtaḇ bəsēfer (and it was written in the book) — the passive construction leaves ambiguous whether this is the book of Esther itself or the chronicles of the Persian court. The self-referential resonance is appropriate: the book of Esther is both the record of the events and the warrant for the feast.
